title: Tajweed Rules for Beginners: The 7 Essential Rules That Transform Your Recitation
excerpt: Tajweed is the science of reciting the Quran with precision and beauty. This beginner's guide breaks down the 7 most important rules with clear examples, so you can start improving your recitation today.
author: QuranBookk
content:
Allah commanded His Prophet ﷺ:

<AyahBlock
  arabic="وَرَتِّلِ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ تَرْتِيلًا"
  translation="And recite the Quran with measured recitation."
  reference="Surah Al-Muzzammil 73:4"
/>

The word *tarteel* in this verse is the Quranic root of "Tajweed" — both refer to careful, precise, beautiful recitation. This is not just an aesthetic preference. Scholars consider learning basic Tajweed an individual obligation (fard 'ayn) on every Muslim who recites the Quran.

## What Is Tajweed?

Tajweed (تجويد) means "to do well" or "to make excellent." As an Islamic science, it is the set of rules governing the correct pronunciation of every letter of the Quran — its point of articulation in the mouth, its characteristics, its interaction with neighbouring letters, and the length of vowels.

When applied correctly, Tajweed gives Quranic recitation its characteristic flowing, melodic quality. When neglected, even small errors can change meanings. The Arabic word قَلْب (qalb = heart) and كَلْب (kalb = dog) differ by one dot — precision is not optional.

## Rule 1: Makharij al-Huruf (Articulation Points)

Every Arabic letter originates from a specific point in the mouth or throat. These are called *makharij* (singular: makhraj). There are 17 primary articulation points across five regions:

- **The throat (al-halq)**: Letters ء ه ع غ ح خ
- **The tongue (al-lisan)**: Most Arabic letters
- **The lips (al-shafatayn)**: ب م و ف
- **The nasal passage (al-khayshum)**: Produces the ghunnah (nasal sound)
- **The empty space (al-jawf)**: Long vowels alif, waw, ya

<Callout type="tip">
The best way to find your makhraj: say the letter with a sukoon (no vowel) and hold it. The point where the sound originates is the makhraj. Your tajweed teacher will guide you through each letter in your first few lessons.
</Callout>

## Rule 2: Noon Sakinah & Tanween (4 Cases)

When a noon with sukoon (نْ) or tanween (ً ٍ ٌ) appears before the next letter, four rules apply depending on which letter follows.

**Idhar (إظهار) — Clear pronunciation**
Before the six throat letters (ء ه ع غ ح خ), pronounce the noon clearly with no merging or prolongation. Example: مِنْ عِلْمٍ (min 'ilmin).

**Idgham (إدغام) — Merging**
Before the letters ي ن م و ل ر, the noon is absorbed into the following letter. If the following letter is ي or و, this happens *with ghunnah*. For ل and ر, the merging is *without ghunnah*. Example: مَن يَقُولُ — the noon disappears into the yaa.

**Iqlab (إقلاب) — Substitution**
Before only the letter ب, the noon transforms into a meem (م) with ghunnah. There is a small مـ written above the noon in the Quran to signal this. Example: مِن بَعْدِ.

**Ikhfa (إخفاء) — Concealment**
Before the remaining 15 letters, the noon is concealed — halfway between clear and merged — with ghunnah. This is the most common rule and the most nuanced to execute correctly.

## Rule 3: Meem Sakinah (3 Cases)

When a meem with sukoon (مْ) appears, three rules apply depending on the next letter.

**Idgham Shafawi** — Before another meem, the meem merges with ghunnah.

**Ikhfa Shafawi** — Before the letter ب, the meem is concealed with ghunnah. This is the only case where ikhfa applies to the meem.

**Idhar Shafawi** — Before all other letters, the meem is pronounced clearly.

## Rule 4: Ghunnah (Nasalisation)

Ghunnah is the nasal sound that accompanies certain letters and situations. The primary letters of ghunnah are noon (ن) and meem (م) — and it occurs in the cases of idgham, ikhfa, iqlab, and when these letters carry a shaddah (ّ).

The standard duration of ghunnah is two counts (harakatayn). Practice by humming with your mouth closed — the vibration in your nose is ghunnah.

## Rule 5: Madd (Lengthening)

Madd governs how long vowels are held. The base rule: every long vowel (alif, waw, ya preceded by a short vowel) is held for 2 counts (the natural madd). Extended cases:

**Madd Muttasil** — When a hamza follows the long vowel *within the same word*. Extended to 4–5 counts. Example: جَاءَ.

**Madd Munfasil** — When a hamza follows the long vowel in the *next word*. Extended to 4–5 counts in careful recitation, some reciters shorten to 2–4. Example: إِنَّا أَعْطَيْنَاكَ.

**Madd Lazim** — The longest madd, at 6 counts. Occurs before a letter with shaddah or sukoon within the same word. Common at the start of certain surahs (المّ, الر).

## Rule 6: Qalqalah (Echo Sound)

Five letters produce a bouncing echo sound when they carry a sukoon or fall at the end of a verse: ق ط ب ج د (collected in the phrase *qutbu jadin*).

The echo is more pronounced at the end of a verse (waqf) and lighter in the middle. Compare how ق sounds in the middle of a word versus at the end of a verse in Surah Al-Ikhlas.

## Rule 7: Stopping Rules (Waqf)

Knowing where and how to stop while reciting is essential — wrong stops can change meaning entirely.

The Quran contains symbols indicating recommended (م), permissible (ج), and forbidden (لا) stopping points. At a stop (waqf), you must:
- Remove any final vowel (fat-ha, kasra, dhamma) — the word ends with sukoon
- Never stop at a tanween — the word changes slightly at waqf (e.g. كِتَابًا becomes كِتَابا)

## Practice Tajweed with QuranBookk

QuranBookk's interactive Quran reader displays Tajweed colour-coding on every letter — immediately showing you where each rule applies as you read. Combined with audio from certified reciters, it is one of the most effective ways to internalise Tajweed rules naturally.

👉 [Practice Tajweed with colour-coded Quran on QuranBookk](/digital-quran)

Tajweed is learned over months and years — but every improvement in your recitation is an act of love for the Book of Allah. Start with one rule, master it, and build from there. The Prophet ﷺ said the one who struggles to recite the Quran correctly gets *double* the reward: once for the recitation and once for the struggle.
